name: APT34
summary: Iranian cyber-espionage threat cluster tracked as OilRig, Hazel Sandstorm, and Earth Simnavaz, active since at least 2014 and focused on Middle Eastern and international government, energy, financial, chemical, telecommunications, and technology-service targets. Tradecraft includes spearphishing and public-facing server compromise, PowerShell/VBScript/.NET loaders, DNS and HTTP C2, Exchange/EWS and OWA abuse, IIS web shells, scheduled-task persistence, credential theft through password filters and mailbox access, and exfiltration through Exchange, FTP, or C2 channels.
extra_hunts: Hunt for DNS tunneling from endpoints or servers: high-volume A/TXT/AAAA lookups, long encoded labels, many unique subdomains per parent domain, requestbin-style tunneling services, and DNS followed by HTTP or Exchange activity.

Review Exchange, OWA, EWS, and IIS telemetry for web shells, suspicious EWS bindings, unusual attachment-based outbound mail, unexpected mailbox access, and web server processes spawning PowerShell, cmd.exe, or .NET loaders.

Inspect scheduled-task persistence using names and paths similar to WindowsAppPool, AppPool, OneDriveStandaloneUpdater, SystemFailureReporter, Office356, or payloads launched every minute or every few minutes.

Hunt for malicious password filter activity: new DLLs under System32, changes to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Notification Packages, and credential theft that continues after password resets.

Pivot from PowerShell, VBScript, macro-enabled Office documents, and .NET droppers into BONDUPDATER, SideTwist/Menorah-like behavior: host fingerprinting, Base64 or custom encoding, shell command execution, file upload, and file download.

Correlate web shell or Exchange compromise with follow-on credential dumping, ngrok or other tunneling tools, external remote-service logins, lateral movement to domain controllers, and exfiltration through Exchange, FTP, or C2.

Treat historical network IOCs as pivot seeds rather than blocking-only indicators; validate surrounding sessions, certificate history, passive DNS, mailbox activity, and host telemetry before escalating.
